system: You are a skeptical reviewer checking a single discovery finding before it reaches a client. You are given the finding and ONLY the evidence it cites (computed numbers + document quotes). Judge whether the stated conclusion is genuinely SUPPORTED by that evidence, or whether it over-reaches — e.g. asserts a causal link from correlational data, generalises beyond what the numbers show, or states something the quotes do not actually say. Default to skepticism: if the evidence does not clearly support the conclusion, mark it unsupported. Reply with strict JSON only: {"supported": true|false, "reason": "...", "suggested_fix": "..."}. suggested_fix is how to reword the finding to match only what the evidence supports (empty if supported).
prompt:
Review this finding:

TITLE: CRM and S/4HANA disagree on credit limits for 267 of 318 customers — €30.7M of aggregate divergence against the policy's sole system of record
CLAIM: An outer join of the SAP S/4HANA customer master against the SAP CRM export on customer_id shows that of 318 matched accounts, 267 have a mismatched credit_limit_eur and 228 have mismatched payment_terms. The summed absolute credit-limit divergence is €30,675,000. The largest single discrepancy is Carrefour France (FR001): ERP €1.8M vs CRM €2.4M (delta €600k), with payment terms also differing (NET45 in ERP vs NET30 in CRM). Boots UK, Tesco UK and E.Leclerc follow the same pattern (€350k, €350k, €300k deltas). The Credit Management Policy states SAP S/4HANA is the SOLE authoritative source for credit limits, yet CS working notes confirm staff are operating to the higher CRM limits. This means credit exposure for the largest accounts cannot be assessed from any single source, and orders may be released against inflated limits.
BUSINESS CONSEQUENCE: Credit decisions and order releases are being made against CRM limits that exceed the ERP system-of-record by up to €600k per account and €30.7M in aggregate, creating unquantified over-extension of credit on the largest retail accounts with no approval trail.
COMPUTED EVIDENCE: Accounts with mismatched credit_limit_eur (of 318 matched)=267; Accounts with mismatched payment_terms=228; Aggregate absolute credit-limit divergence (EUR)=30675000.0; Carrefour France ERP credit limit (EUR)=1800000.0; Carrefour France CRM credit limit (EUR)=2400000.0; Carrefour France largest single delta (EUR)=600000.0
DOCUMENT EVIDENCE: Policy names ERP as sole authoritative source: “Single system of record: SAP S/4HANA is the sole authoritative source for all customer credit limits and”; AR review confirms Carrefour split limits: “at — CRM has EUR 2,400,000 and ERP has EUR 1,800,000. This is a material difference. The”; Policy does not define authoritative system in practice: “record. Our credit policy does not define which system is authoritative. As a result,”
response: {"supported": false, "reason": "The document evidence partly contradicts the claim. The policy quote says S/4HANA is 'the sole authoritative source,' but another quoted CS/AR note explicitly states 'Our credit policy does not define which system is authoritative' — these conflict, undermining the finding's assertion of a clear 'sole system of record.' Also, the claim that 'staff are operating to the higher CRM limits' and 'orders may be released against inflated limits' is not supported by any quoted evidence; no working note confirming staff behavior or order releases is shown. The Boots/Tesco/E.Leclerc deltas (€350k, €350k, €300k) have no computed evidence backing them. The aggregate €30.7M and the 267/228 mismatch counts and Carrefour figures are supported, but the operational/behavioral conclusions and the 'sole source' framing over-reach.", "suggested_fix": "Reframe to: An outer join shows 267 of 318 matched accounts have mismatched credit limits (€30,675,000 aggregate absolute divergence) and 228 have mismatched payment terms; the largest verified case is Carrefour France (ERP €1.8M vs CRM €2.4M, €600k delta). The Credit Management Policy nominally names S/4HANA as authoritative, but internal notes indicate the policy does not define which system is authoritative in practice, creating ambiguity about which limit governs credit decisions. Drop unverified Boots/Tesco/E.Leclerc figures and the unsupported claims that staff are operating to CRM limits or releasing orders against inflated limits unless corroborating evidence is cited."}
